blocks.text: Re: Advising Scott, Smith and Edwards on its digital operating model
Dear Ms. Johnson:

This letter sets down the terms on which we will help you rebuild the way your online business is run. The question you have put to us is a narrow one, and I will state it plainly: the share of your orders that now comes through the web channel has outgrown the organization behind it, and you want that organization matched to what the channel has become. What follows describes the work we will do to answer that, the people who will do it, what we will need from you, and the terms that govern the engagement. Once you have signed and returned this letter, it becomes the agreement between us and it takes the place of anything discussed before it.
The Work

We will deliver three things. The first is a diagnostic of how {{fact:f:E-2011-001.client}} runs its digital business today: where orders are taken and where they are filled, which functions own which parts of the channel, how customer and order information moves between the systems that hold it, and what the present arrangement costs to operate. The second is a target operating model, a design that fixes ownership of the online channel, sets out the decisions each role is accountable for, and puts around them the governance a channel of this size now requires. The third is a roadmap that sequences the change from where you are to that model and shows where the effort returns the most.

Some matters sit outside this work, and I will name them here so they are not left to assumption. We will recommend where roles and accountabilities should change; the decisions about individual people remain yours, and we do not carry them out. Selecting or installing software, negotiating with your technology vendors, and the tax or legal treatment of any reorganization are not part of this engagement. Where our analysis meets a question we have not been retained to answer, we will say so and stop there instead of guessing at it.
How We Will Proceed

We begin with your own figures, your own systems, and your own people, not with a design carried in from another company. In the opening weeks we will map how the channel actually operates, interview the managers who run it day to day, and assemble a plain account of the cost and the hand-offs as they stand. From that base we design the target model and test it against the demands the business will place on it over the next few years. We form a view and we state it; we do not hand you a menu of options and ask you to do the designing yourselves.
Who Will Do the Work

Jason Torres, a Principal of the firm, leads the engagement and is your point of contact for anything touching the design or the schedule. Deborah Allen, Consultant, builds the current-state picture and the cost analysis and will run the exchange of data and definitions with your team; she is the person to reach on the numbers and the systems detail. I review the analysis and the recommendation before either is put in front of you, as I do with all of the firm's work. We will not change the people on your engagement without telling you first.
Fee and Payment

Our fee for the work described above is a fixed {{fact:f:E-2011-001.fee}}, which covers the diagnostic, the target model, and the roadmap, together with the team's ordinary travel to and from your offices. We invoice in three equal installments across the engagement, each payable within thirty days of the invoice date. The fee is fixed against the scope set out in this letter. If you later ask us for work this letter does not describe, we will price it and wait for your written approval before we take it up; we do not add to the bill on our own reading of what you meant.
When We Begin

Work begins on {{fact:f:E-2011-001.start}}, once we have your countersignature and the materials the team will need to start: your current organization chart for the channel and the functions around it, a list of the systems that carry order and customer data, and the names of the managers we should interview. If any of that will take time to gather, tell Deborah Allen and we will sequence the first weeks around what is ready.
Term and Ending the Engagement

This agreement takes effect on the date you sign below and runs until we have delivered the roadmap and you have paid our final invoice. Either of us may end it earlier on thirty days' written notice to the other. If the engagement ends before it is finished, you will pay us for the work done and the expenses incurred to that point, and we will hand over what we have produced in the state it then stands, with a written note of what is left open. An unfinished diagnostic carries no recommendation, and none should be drawn from it.
Handling Your Information

We will treat everything you give us as confidential and use it only for this engagement. Inside the firm, access is limited to the team named above and to those whose work requires it. We will not pass your information to anyone outside the firm without your written consent, unless a court or a regulator with authority compels it, and in that case we will tell you first where we are permitted to. What we produce is prepared for your management and is not to be used on another matter without our agreement in writing. This obligation outlasts the engagement itself.
The Limit of Our Liability

Our total liability to you arising from this engagement, whether a claim is framed in contract or in tort, will not exceed the fees you pay us under it, save in the case of our own fraud or willful misconduct. Neither party will be liable to the other for indirect or consequential loss, lost profits among them, whether or not the possibility was raised. Our conclusions rest on the accuracy of what you give us; where that information proves materially wrong, the consequences of the error are not ours to bear, though we will tell you at once if we see reason to doubt what we have been handed.
Governing Law and Disputes

This agreement is governed by the laws of the state in which the firm's principal office sits, without regard to its conflict-of-laws rules. Before either of us starts formal proceedings, we will try to settle any dispute through discussion between senior people on both sides. This letter is the whole of the agreement between us on its subject and may be changed only in writing signed by us both.

If these terms match your understanding, please sign both copies where shown below, keep one for your records, and return the other to me. If anything in them does not, call me before you sign and we will settle it then. We are glad to be taking this on with Scott, Smith and Edwards, and the team will be ready to begin the interviews as soon as the signed letter is back with us.
Very truly yours,

To: Jason Torres and the Scott, Smith and Edwards engagement team
From: Deborah Allen
Re: Digital operating model for {{fact:f:E-2011-001.client}} — how we are setting up the work

We started on {{fact:f:E-2011-001.start}}, so this note sets down what we are answering and how the first weeks divide. The client's problem is a specific one. The web channel now carries a real share of the orders at {{fact:f:E-2011-001.client}}, but the organization behind it was built when the channel was a side experiment, and the seams show: orders are taken in one place and filled in another, customer questions land on a team that cannot see the order, and no single manager owns the channel from end to end. Our job is to describe that plainly, design the operating model the channel now needs, and lay out how to get there. Melissa Johnson, the General Manager, is our sponsor on the client side and the person who will carry the recommendation to her leadership.
How the work divides

items: Current-state map. I own this. We trace one order from the moment a customer places it online to the moment it ships, and we mark every hand-off, every system it passes through, and every point where someone keys the same information a second time.

The cost of running it today. I build a plain view of what the channel costs to operate across the functions that touch it, so that later, when we propose to move work or ownership, the saving or the cost is a number the client already recognizes.

Ownership and decisions. We map who decides what across marketing, operations, and the technology group: who sets the online assortment, who owns the delivery promise, who answers for the channel's result at the end of the month. This is where I expect the real problem to sit.

Systems and data. Jason Torres and I inventory the systems that hold order and customer data and how they pass it between one another. We are documenting what exists, not recommending a replacement; software selection is outside our scope and stays there.

blocks.text: The first two weeks

items: I send the client a single, itemized data request this week: the channel organization chart, the last year of order and cost figures for the online business, and a short list of the systems involved. One request, not a trickle of them.
We agree the interview list before we ask for a single calendar slot, so we are not renegotiating access once we are on site. I will bring a draft to our first team meeting.
Jason Torres confirms the on-site dates with Melissa Johnson's office and holds a half day of slack for the interviews that always move.
Nothing about a target structure goes to anyone at the client until the current-state map reconciles. An early picture is wrong often enough that showing a wrong one is hard to take back.

blocks.text: My own read, before we have gathered a thing, is that this will prove to be a question of ownership more than of systems. The technology is stitched together, but companies live with stitched-together systems; what they cannot live with is no one being able to say who owns the channel's number at the end of the month. I would like us to test that early, because if it holds it shapes everything we design. If it does not, we will know that early and can design for what we actually find.
Digital Operating Model — {{fact:f:E-2011-001.client}}

items: A working briefing for Melissa Johnson, General Manager
Engagement began {{fact:f:E-2011-001.start}}
Prepared by Deborah Allen, with Jason Torres, Principal
Early-phase read: how we are set up and what we see so far, not recommendations

blocks.text: What you asked us to answer

items: Match the organization behind the web channel to the volume it now carries
Fix who owns the online channel end to end, and who decides what along it
Show what the channel costs to run today, in numbers your own finance team recognizes
Lay out a practical path from where you are to where the channel needs to be

blocks.text: How we are working

items: We trace a real order from the web page to the shipping dock and mark every hand-off along the way
We start from your charts, your systems, and your own figures, not from a template
Deborah Allen builds the current-state map and the cost picture
Jason Torres owns the design and is your day-to-day contact

blocks.text: What we have set up so far

items: The data request is out, and the first order and cost figures are in and reconciling
Interviews with the channel managers are scheduled through the coming weeks
A first draft of the order path exists and is being checked against how the work is really done
No target structure is on the table yet, and none should be read into this briefing

blocks.text: What comes next

items: Finish the interviews and close the current-state map
Turn the cost picture into a view by function, so spend can be read against who owns it
Bring the first findings to the next working session for discussion
Questions on the design to Jason Torres; anything on the data or systems to Deborah Allen

blocks.text: Melissa Johnson, with Jason Torres copied:

A short update on where we are and one thing I need from your team. The order and cost figures your office sent came through cleanly, and I have started laying out how a web order moves through {{fact:f:E-2011-001.client}} from the moment a customer places it. The picture is taking shape: the hand-off between the group that takes the order and the group that fills it is where most of the delay collects, which lines up with what your managers told us in the first interviews.

The one gap is the customer-service side. I have the order data and the fulfillment data, but not the record of the questions and complaints raised against those orders, and that record is what tells us whether a problem is a system fault or an ownership gap. If someone on your team can pull the last quarter of service tickets tied to online orders by the end of the week, we stay on schedule for the working session. Jason will be on site Thursday and can take anything that is easier to hand over in person than to send.

Nothing else is needed from you today. I will send the draft order-path map ahead of the session so you are not seeing it cold in the room.

Deborah Allen
Melissa Johnson, Jason Torres still copied:

Thank you for turning the service tickets around so quickly. They arrived last night and they already answer part of the question I had. Against the online orders at {{fact:f:E-2011-001.client}}, the complaints cluster in the day after an order is placed, and most of them are people asking where their order is, not people reporting something wrong with it. That points at the hand-off again rather than at the fulfillment itself, and it is a useful thing to be able to say with the ticket data behind it instead of on impression alone.

One follow-up while your team still has the files open. Where a ticket was escalated, I would like to see who it went to, because that shows where the channel's problems land when no one owns them by design. Even a rough note against a dozen recent escalations would do; I do not need the whole quarter marked up.

Jason and I will have the order-path map and this first cut of the service picture ready for the working session next week. If the escalation notes are difficult to pull, say so and we will work from what we already have.

Deborah Allen
Working Session: Digital Operating Model — First Findings
Minutes taken by Jason Torres. Subject: the digital operating model engagement for {{fact:f:E-2011-001.client}}, first working session with the client team.
In the room

items: Jason Torres, Principal, Advisory
Deborah Allen, Consultant, Advisory
Melissa Johnson, General Manager, Scott, Smith and Edwards

blocks.text: Where the work stands

I opened by stating the finding, because it is already clear enough to build on: the channel's problem is ownership, not technology. We are roughly at the midpoint of the work, the current-state map is nearly closed, and everywhere it is closed it points the same way. Orders are taken by one group and filled by another, and between them sits a hand-off that no single manager owns. Deborah Allen walked the table through the order path she has drawn, one screen at a time, and the group followed it against a set of real orders from last month. The delay and the complaints both collect at the same seam, the point where the order leaves the team that sold it and reaches the team that ships it, and nobody in the room could name the person who owns the order while it sits in that gap.
What the numbers say

Deborah Allen took the group through the cost and service data, and two figures did most of the arguing. The service tickets tied to online orders cluster in the day after purchase, and most are customers asking where their order is rather than reporting a fault, which puts the cost of the seam in plain view: the channel is paying people to answer for a delay it could have prevented. And the cost of running the channel is spread across three functions, with none of them accountable for the total, which is why, Melissa Johnson noted, the monthly number has been so hard for her office to close. She said her finance team can produce any one function's cost on request but has never been asked to add them into a single figure for the channel, because no one has owned that figure. I said what I think follows from all of it: until one role owns the channel result end to end, the company keeps paying for the gap in service cost and in rework, and no change to the systems fixes that on its own. The table did not argue the point, which I take as agreement rather than as the end of the discussion.
What we settled and what stays open

We agreed the current-state map is close enough to brief the wider leadership, with the last two interviews still to be folded in. Melissa Johnson asked us to hold any talk of a specific structure until the target model is designed and tested, and I agreed; a structure floated early gets argued about before it is understood, and we would spend the back half of the engagement defending a sketch instead of building the real design. Two questions stay open. The first is the technology group's place in the target model, which we will not settle until the design work is done. The second is how far the ownership problem reaches beyond the web channel into the rest of the order business; the interviews hint that it does, and we will test that before we draw the target model rather than assume it.
Who does what next

rows: Fold the last two channel interviews into the current-state map and close it
Pull the escalation notes on recent online orders and tie them to the cost view
Draft the two or three target-model options, for discussion and not for decision
Keep the current-state findings inside this group until the leadership briefing
